entry_points.reachable_from: local conformance runner writes a 4-byte request length and serialized ConformanceRequest to the testee stdin
conformance_test_runner reads the 4-byte response length from the testee stdout and trusts it for the following payload read

consequences.description: Once the oversized first response is advertised as 4 bytes instead of 4294967300 bytes, the next 4 leftover payload bytes are reinterpreted as the next frame header. A valid subsequent response is swallowed into a corrupted frame payload and the stream loses synchronization.

If the leftover payload bytes decode to a large integer, the parent process trusts that size and waits for that many bytes. In the real runner this becomes a blocking CheckedRead() call that ends in timeout or unexpected EOF handling.
consequences.evidence: impact_output.txt scenario 'service_disruption': reader_next_length_from_leftover_payload=268435456 with only 9 bytes remaining, missing_bytes_before_second_frame_completes=268435447

consequences.description: The poisoned next-frame length can coerce the parent into attempting a very large response buffer before reading. ForkPipeRunner::RunTest() resizes the response string to the attacker-influenced length before CheckedRead().
consequences.evidence: targets/protobuf/conformance/fork_pipe_runner.cc:115-117 performs response.resize(len) after trusting the 32-bit length read from the child stream

demonstration.summary: The demo replays the real conformance framing contract and shows two practical outcomes immediately after a 4 GiB+ response is truncated to a 32-bit header: stream desynchronization that swallows a valid next frame, and a poisoned next-frame length that would make the parent block or fail while waiting for hundreds of megabytes that were never part of a real frame.

notes: Impact is constrained to the conformance test harness boundary, not normal protobuf parsing APIs.

Triggering the original truncation requires producing a serialized ConformanceResponse larger than 4 GiB, so exploitation is impractical for ordinary conformance runs but still causes protocol breakage if reached.
